persons.arguments_against: Conroy is moderately common (8 officials, 5 bios), so a same-name relative sharing the J. G. initials and the colony is the counter-hypothesis. But the office is specific and singular -- 'Central District Court Judge, Newfoundland' (position_sim 100) -- and the bio and stint co-occur across an unusually long span: the bio is printed in 1888-1917 and the stint runs 1889-1915 with matching position, including same-edition agreement in 1889 and 1894. The bio's 1880 appointment precedes the stint's 1889 first listing consistently (staff-list coverage starting later). For the counter-hypothesis to hold, two J. G. Conroys would both have to be Central District Court Judges of Newfoundland across the same two-plus decades -- not credible. Objection fails.

evidence_cited.fact: J. G. Conroy | Central District Court Judge
Central District Court Judges | Judicial Establishment

persons.would_overturn: A printed record of a second J. G. Conroy also holding a Central District Court judgeship in Newfoundland, or a death date for the bio incompatible with listing through 1915.

notes: Two-initial match, singular specific judicial office, and long-span edition co-occurrence (1889-1915) with position_sim 100. No live counter-hypothesis.
